title: Food–drug interaction guidance
description: Baked per-item food–drug guidance (issue #154, extends #144) — the food note shown on a medication/supplement card and dose reminder. CURATED HIGH-VALUE SUBSET, not exhaustive; facts from public-domain FDA/DailyMed labeling, NIH MedlinePlus/NLM, and RxNorm ingredient concepts (all public domain). INFORMATIONAL, never prescriptive — absence of an entry does NOT mean a food is safe with a drug. Committed + HUMAN-REVIEWABLE; regenerate with `npm run gen:food-drug-interactions`.

citation.source: FDA Structured Product Labeling / DailyMed drug-interaction and patient-counseling sections; NIH MedlinePlus / NLM; RxNorm (NLM) ingredient concepts.
citation.note: All public-domain / public U.S. references; each rule additionally carries its own per-rule `source`. Curated high-value subset, not exhaustive.

entries.advice: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice — they raise statin blood levels and the risk of muscle injury.
entries.mechanism: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4, slowing statin breakdown so more drug reaches the bloodstream and myopathy/rhabdomyolysis becomes more likely.

entries.advice: Avoid grapefruit juice — it can sharply raise blood levels and cause a large drop in blood pressure.
entries.mechanism: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4, increasing exposure to these dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers and the risk of hypotension.

entries.advice: Avoid grapefruit — it sharply raises drug levels and the risk of toxicity.
entries.mechanism: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4, increasing exposure to these narrow-therapeutic-index immunosuppressants.

entries.advice: Keep vitamin K intake steady — don't suddenly increase leafy greens (kale, spinach, broccoli); big swings change how well warfarin works.
entries.mechanism: Vitamin K directly opposes warfarin's anticoagulant effect, so inconsistent dietary intake destabilizes the INR.

entries.advice: Limit alcohol — it can unpredictably raise or lower warfarin's effect and add to bleeding risk.
entries.mechanism: Alcohol alters warfarin metabolism (acutely inhibiting, chronically inducing) and independently impairs clotting, destabilizing the INR.

entries.food: Coffee, dairy, calcium, and high-fiber foods
entries.advice: Take on an empty stomach with water 30–60 min before eating; keep coffee, dairy, calcium, and iron ≥4 hours apart — they cut absorption.
entries.mechanism: Food, coffee, and polyvalent cations (calcium, iron) bind levothyroxine in the gut and reduce how much is absorbed, lowering its effect.

entries.advice: Don't take with dairy, calcium, iron, or antacids — they bind the antibiotic and can make it fail. Separate by ≥2 hours.
entries.mechanism: Calcium, magnesium, iron, and other polyvalent cations chelate tetracyclines in the gut, sharply reducing absorption.

entries.food: Dairy, calcium, iron, magnesium, and antacids
entries.advice: Separate from dairy, calcium, iron, magnesium, and antacids by ≥2 hours — they bind the antibiotic and reduce absorption.
entries.mechanism: Polyvalent cations chelate fluoroquinolones in the gut, markedly reducing absorption and antibacterial effect.

entries.advice: Avoid all alcohol during treatment and for 3 days after — the combination causes flushing, nausea, vomiting, and a racing heartbeat.
entries.mechanism: Metronidazole inhibits acetaldehyde dehydrogenase, producing a disulfiram-like reaction when alcohol is consumed.

entries.food: Tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese, cured meats, draft beer)
entries.advice: Avoid tyramine-rich foods — aged cheeses, cured or smoked meats, fermented soy, and draft beer — they can trigger a dangerous blood-pressure spike.
entries.mechanism: MAO inhibitors block the breakdown of dietary tyramine, which can release stored norepinephrine and cause a hypertensive crisis.

entries.advice: Go easy on potassium-based salt substitutes and large amounts of high-potassium foods — with an ACE inhibitor/ARB they can push potassium too high.
entries.mechanism: ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce potassium excretion, so a big potassium load (especially salt substitutes) can cause hyperkalemia.

entries.advice: Avoid potassium-based salt substitutes and go easy on high-potassium foods — these diuretics already raise potassium and the combination can become dangerous.
entries.mechanism: Potassium-sparing diuretics retain potassium, so an added dietary potassium load can cause hyperkalemia and arrhythmia.

entries.advice: Avoid regular or heavy alcohol — combined with acetaminophen it raises the risk of liver damage. Stay within the daily acetaminophen limit.
entries.mechanism: Chronic alcohol induces CYP2E1, increasing the toxic acetaminophen metabolite (NAPQI) and the risk of liver injury.

entries.food: Any food or drink except plain water; calcium and coffee
entries.advice: Take first thing in the morning with plain water only, stay upright, and wait ≥30 minutes before any food, coffee, or calcium — food blocks nearly all absorption.
entries.mechanism: Oral bisphosphonates are very poorly absorbed; any food, coffee, or calcium in the gut reduces the already-low absorption to almost nothing.
